title: Scalar types and declarations

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Scalar types and declarations; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. canonicalExplanation.summary: C scalar types include integer, real floating, complex where supported, Boolean, enumeration, and pointer types, each with implementation-defined representation and operation rules.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: An object count uses size_t because it represents object sizes and array bounds rather than assuming unsigned int has the same range. Your goal is to choose and declare a suitable scalar type while avoiding unjustified size assumptions. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Translation unit and main; pf state expressions. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: C scalar types include integer, real floating, complex where supported, Boolean, enumeration, and pointer types, each with implementation-defined representation and operation rules. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Widths, signed representation details, plain char signedness, floating formats, alignment, and pointer representation vary by implementation despite minimum standard guarantees. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: C guarantees int is exactly 32 bits and plain char is always signed on every supported system.

includes: Use integer, floating, Boolean, character and void-related declarations appropriately.
Choose and declare a suitable scalar type while avoiding unjustified size assumptions.
excludes: Widths, signed representation details, plain char signedness, floating formats, alignment, and pointer representation vary by implementation despite minimum standard guarantees.

outcomes: Choose and declare a suitable scalar type while avoiding unjustified size assumptions.

examples.title: Scalar types and declarations: defined case
examples.situation: An object count uses size_t because it represents object sizes and array bounds rather than assuming unsigned int has the same range.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: An object count uses size_t because it represents object sizes and array bounds rather than assuming unsigned int has the same range.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Use limits and fixed-width types only when their documented guarantees fit; sizeof and the implementation headers describe actual scalar ranges and widths.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Use limits and fixed-width types only when their documented guarantees fit; sizeof and the implementation headers describe actual scalar ranges and widths.

examples.title: Scalar types and declarations: boundary case
examples.situation: A byte read through unsigned char preserves all possible object-representation byte values without negative plain-char surprises.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A byte read through unsigned char preserves all possible object-representation byte values without negative plain-char surprises.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Widths, signed representation details, plain char signedness, floating formats, alignment, and pointer representation vary by implementation despite minimum standard guarantees.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: C guarantees int is exactly 32 bits and plain char is always signed on every supported system.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Choose and declare a suitable scalar type while avoiding unjustified size assumptions.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: C guarantees int is exactly 32 bits and plain char is always signed on every supported system.
misconceptions.correction: Use limits and fixed-width types only when their documented guarantees fit; sizeof and the implementation headers describe actual scalar ranges and widths.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: C guarantees int is exactly 32 bits and plain char is always signed on every supported system.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Scalar types and declarations: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
Widths, signed representation details, plain char signedness, floating formats, alignment, and pointer representation vary by implementation despite minimum standard guarantees.
